meta.title: Vedic Maths Mastery: Speed Calculation with All 16 Sutras
meta.description: Vedic maths course online with live classes: all 16 sutras for fast multiplication, squares, division and mental calculation. Ages 8 to adult, free demo class.
meta.commitment: 2 live classes/week + 20-30 minutes of drill practice most days
meta.certification: Course-completion certificate from Modern Age Coders

program_overview.title: Vedic Maths Mastery
program_overview.tagline: The same sums, done in a third of the time, with a method you can check.
program_overview.description:
Vedic maths is a system of calculation shortcuts organised around 16 sutras, short working rules like Nikhilam (all from 9 and the last from 10) and Urdhva-Tiryagbhyam (vertically and crosswise). Used well, they turn sums like 97 x 96 or 998 squared into a few seconds of mental work. This 6-month live course teaches the system the way a maths teacher would: every shortcut is shown, practised against the clock, and then explained, because a student who knows why a trick works can trust it in an exam and adapt it when the numbers change.

The course runs in one straight line: multiplication shortcuts first, because they pay off fastest, then squares and cubes, then division including the flag method, then fractions, decimals and percentages at speed, and finally the algebra applications of the same sutras. Week 1 starts with a timed diagnostic; week 24 ends with the same style of paper, so every student can see exactly how much faster and more accurate they have become. We are honest about what this course is: it makes arithmetic faster and frees up exam time. It does not replace school maths, and it will not turn anyone into a prodigy. It rewards steady drill, and we structure that drill for you.
what_makes_this_different: Every shortcut comes with its reason: students see the algebra behind Nikhilam and the duplex method, so the rules stick instead of jumbling together
Timed drills from week 1, with a personal speed log so progress is measured against your own diagnostic, not against a claim
We teach judgement, not just tricks: which sutra fits which sum, and when the ordinary school method is actually the better choice
Digit-sum checking is built in from the start, so speed never comes at the cost of accuracy
Mixed-age friendly: the same system is drilled at school-exam pace for children and at competitive-exam pace for older students
Live small-group classes where the teacher watches you calculate, catches bad habits early, and adjusts the drill sheets to your errors

learning_path.phase_1: The multiplication engine: complements, Nikhilam base multiplication, special patterns, then Urdhva-Tiryagbhyam for any numbers, all against the clock
learning_path.phase_2: Squares, cubes and roots with Yavadunam and the duplex method, then division three ways: Nikhilam, Paravartya and the flag method
learning_path.phase_3: Fractions, decimals and percentages at speed, the algebra applications of the sutras, and a timed capstone measured against the week 1 diagnostic

career_outcomes: Noticeably faster, more accurate arithmetic, with the improvement documented in a personal speed log
Real time savings in school exams and the arithmetic-heavy sections of competitive exams
A checking habit (digit sums) that cuts careless errors in all written maths
A personal shortcut handbook, written by the student, covering every technique in the course
Stronger number sense that carries into algebra, since half the sutras are algebra in disguise

phase_1_multiplication_engine.title: PHASE 1: The Multiplication Engine (Months 1-2, Weeks 1-8)
phase_1_multiplication_engine.description: Multiplication shortcuts pay off fastest, so they come first: complements and base multiplication, the special patterns, then Urdhva-Tiryagbhyam for any two numbers.

week_1.title: How the System Works: Complements
topics: Timed diagnostic paper: 30 mixed sums, your before picture for the whole course
What the 16 sutras are and how this course walks through them
Nikhilam Navatashcaramam Dashatah: all from 9 and the last from 10
Instant subtraction from 100, 1000 and 10000 using complements
Left-to-right working: why Vedic methods read numbers the way we say them
Digit sums (Beejank) as a ten-second answer check
projects: Personal speed log opened: diagnostic scores recorded by sum type, to be updated all course
week_1.practice: 40 complement subtractions from 100, 1000 and 10000, target under 5 minutes with every answer digit-sum checked

week_2.title: Nikhilam Multiplication Below the Base
topics: Multiplying numbers just under 100: 97 x 96 in one line
The two-part answer: cross subtraction on the left, deficiency product on the right
Base 1000 and base 10: 988 x 997, 8 x 7 done the Nikhilam way
Handling carries when the right-hand part overflows
Why it works: the algebra of (base - a)(base - b), shown honestly
Spotting when a sum is close enough to a base for Nikhilam to be worth it
projects: One-page proof poster: a student's own explanation of why Nikhilam works, with one worked example per base
week_2.practice: 30 Nikhilam multiplications near 100 and 1000, target under 8 minutes, misses redone with the algebra written out

week_3.title: Above the Base and Working Bases
topics: Numbers just above the base: 104 x 103, 1002 x 1015
Mixed cases, one above and one below: 105 x 97 and the negative right-hand part
Vinculum digits: writing 98 as 100 minus 2 inside a calculation
Anurupyena, the working base idea: multiplying near 50, 200, 250 and 500
Choosing the base that makes the sum smallest
Speed drill technique: reading the sum, picking the method, then writing
projects: Worked set: 20 mixed base multiplications with the chosen base and method noted next to each
week_3.practice: 35 mixed above/below/working-base multiplications, target under 10 minutes, with the base choice justified for any five

week_4.title: The Special Patterns
topics: Ekadhikena Purvena: squares of numbers ending in 5, done in two seconds
Antyayordashake'pi: same start, last digits adding to 10, such as 43 x 47
Multiplying by 11 and by 12 with single-pass rules
Multiplying by 5, 25 and 50 as fractions of powers of 10
Pattern recognition drills: which of the five patterns fits, or none
Consolidation of month 1: complements, Nikhilam, working bases, patterns
projects: Pattern wall chart: all month 1 shortcuts on one sheet in the student's own words and examples
week_4.practice: 50-question pattern-spotting sheet mixing all month 1 techniques, target under 15 minutes
week_4.assessment: Month 1 speed check: timed mixed paper, scores logged against the week 1 diagnostic

month_2_general_multiplication.title: Month 2: Urdhva-Tiryagbhyam, Multiplication for Any Numbers

week_5.title: Vertically and Crosswise: Two Digits
topics: Urdhva-Tiryagbhyam for 2-digit x 2-digit: the three-step cross pattern
Where each part of the answer comes from, mapped to long multiplication
Carry discipline: keeping the running answer clean
One-line working: 34 x 72 without intermediate rows
Checking with digit sums after every sum, as a habit not an afterthought
When Nikhilam beats Urdhva-Tiryagbhyam and the reverse
projects: Cross-pattern diagram set: the student draws the crosswise structure for five sums before solving them
week_5.practice: 40 two-digit by two-digit crosswise multiplications, target under 12 minutes at 90 percent accuracy

week_6.title: Three Digits and Mixed Sizes
topics: The five-step cross pattern for 3-digit x 3-digit
Mixed sizes: 3-digit x 2-digit by padding with a zero
Managing bigger carries without losing the thread
Writing less: moving from full working to compact working
Common crosswise errors and how the digit-sum check catches them
Speed strategy: which digit pairs to multiply first
projects: Worked set: 15 three-digit crosswise multiplications with compact working shown
week_6.practice: 25 three-digit crosswise multiplications, target under 15 minutes, every answer digit-sum checked

week_7.title: Taking It Mental
topics: Dropping the pen: holding the cross pattern in your head for 2-digit sums
Chunking: multiplying 68 x 7 as 60 x 7 plus 8 x 7, said aloud
Doubling and halving: 16 x 35 as 8 x 70
Multiplying by 125, 75 and 15 through fraction thinking
Estimation first: knowing roughly what the answer must be before starting
Building a personal mental-maths warm-up routine
projects: Mental drill routine card: a 5-minute daily warm-up the student designs and follows
week_7.practice: Daily 5-minute no-paper drills: 20 two-digit multiplications spoken or typed, streaks recorded in the speed log

week_8.title: Speed Lab One
topics: Full multiplication mix under exam timing: patterns, bases, crosswise, mental
Error triage: was it method choice, a carry slip, or rushing
Reading your speed log: what actually got faster since week 1
Accuracy floors: why we never trade below 90 percent accuracy for speed
Personal weak-spot drill sheets, built from your own error log
Setting the phase 2 targets with the teacher
projects: Speed lab paper one: a timed 60-question multiplication paper, marked and logged the same day
week_8.practice: Redo every speed lab miss untimed with full working, then a second 20-question timed set on just those types
week_8.assessment: Phase 1 milestone: timed multiplication paper compared line by line with the week 1 diagnostic

phase_2_squares_cubes_division.title: PHASE 2: Squares, Cubes and Division (Months 3-4, Weeks 9-16)
phase_2_squares_cubes_division.description: Powers and roots with Yavadunam and the duplex method, then the part most courses skip: division done three ways, including Tirthaji's general flag method.

week_9.title: Squares Near a Base
topics: Yavadunam: squaring by deficiency, 98 squared in one line
Squaring just above the base: 104 squared, 1012 squared
Working bases again: 48 squared using base 50
The algebra behind it: (base + d) squared, expanded and matched to the rule
Squares of numbers ending in 5 revisited and extended to 3 digits
Choosing between Yavadunam and the ending-in-5 rule
projects: Worked set: squares of every number from 91 to 109 by the fastest route, method noted for each
week_9.practice: 30 near-base squares across bases 50, 100, 500 and 1000, target under 10 minutes

week_10.title: The Duplex Method: Any Square
topics: The duplex (Dwandwa) of a number: definition and drill
Squaring any 2-digit number with duplexes
Squaring 3-digit numbers: the five duplex terms in order
Keeping carries straight in duplex work
Duplex squares against near-base squares: picking the faster tool
Digit-sum checking squares, including the common square-check traps
projects: Duplex table: the student builds a reference table of duplexes for 1 to 3 digit forms with examples
week_10.practice: 25 general squares of 2 and 3 digit numbers by duplex, target under 12 minutes at 90 percent accuracy

week_11.title: Square Roots and Cube Patterns
topics: Square roots of perfect squares: pairing digits and reading the first digit
Last-digit analysis: which digits can end a perfect square and what they tell you
Deciding between the two candidate roots quickly
Cubes near a base: 98 cubed and 102 cubed by the Nikhilam-style rule
The proportional (Anurupyena) cube rule for 2-digit numbers
Recognising perfect squares and cubes on sight
projects: Root-finder sheet: 20 perfect squares up to 5 digits, roots found and verified by squaring back
week_11.practice: 20 square roots of perfect squares and 10 near-base cubes, target under 15 minutes

week_12.title: Cube Roots and the Powers Checkpoint
topics: Cube roots of perfect cubes up to 6 digits from the two ends
Why cube last digits are honest: the 0 to 9 cube-ending map
Mixed powers drill: squares, cubes and roots on one sheet
Where powers shortcuts show up in school exams and MCQ papers
Month 3 consolidation: one method chart for all powers work
Speed log review: powers section opened and baselined
projects: Powers method chart: every squares, cubes and roots technique on one page with a worked example each
week_12.practice: Mixed 40-question powers paper covering all month 3 techniques, target under 18 minutes
week_12.assessment: Month 3 milestone: timed powers paper, results logged and weak types tagged for drilling

week_13.title: Nikhilam Division
topics: Dividing by 9: the pattern that starts the whole method
Nikhilam division with divisors just below a base: 89, 98, 888
Quotient and remainder columns, and moving carries between them
When the remainder column overflows and how to fix it
Why it works: complements doing subtraction's job in division
Speed comparison against long division on the same sums
projects: Side-by-side set: 10 divisions worked both by long division and Nikhilam with times recorded for each
week_13.practice: 25 Nikhilam divisions with divisors near 10, 100 and 1000, target under 12 minutes

week_14.title: Paravartya: Transpose and Apply
topics: Paravartya Yojayet for divisors just above a base: 12, 112, 1023
Transposing digits: turning the divisor's excess into negative multipliers
Working with negative intermediate digits without panic
Nikhilam or Paravartya: reading the divisor and choosing in two seconds
Remainder checks with digit sums
The algebra behind Paravartya, shown for divisor 12
projects: Choice drill sheet: 20 divisions labelled Nikhilam or Paravartya before solving, choices reviewed in class
week_14.practice: 25 Paravartya divisions with divisors near 10, 100 and 1000, target under 14 minutes

week_15.title: Straight Division: The Flag Method
topics: Dhvajanka, the flag digit: general division for any divisor
Setting up the flag: splitting the divisor into main digit and flag
The subtract-and-divide rhythm, worked slowly then at pace
Two-digit flags for harder divisors
Decimal answers: continuing the division past the point
Why the flag method matters: one method that always works
projects: Worked set: 12 flag-method divisions including two carried to three decimal places
week_15.practice: 20 flag-method divisions with mixed divisors from 13 to 87, target under 20 minutes

week_16.title: Divisibility and Speed Lab Two
topics: Divisibility tests for 2 through 12, consolidated and drilled
Osculation: Ekadhikena-based tests for 7, 13, 19 and other awkward primes
Remainder thinking: Navashesh checks for division answers
Full division mix under time: Nikhilam, Paravartya, flag, divisibility
Error triage on division work
Phase 3 targets set with the teacher
projects: Speed lab paper two: a timed 50-question powers and division paper, marked and logged the same day
week_16.practice: Redo all speed lab misses untimed, then 15 divisibility questions on primes 7, 13 and 19 by osculation
week_16.assessment: Phase 2 milestone: timed powers and division paper, logged against months 3 and 4 baselines

phase_3_speed_maths_in_use.title: PHASE 3: Fractions, Algebra and the Capstone (Months 5-6, Weeks 17-24)
phase_3_speed_maths_in_use.description: The system applied where exams actually spend marks: fractions, decimals and percentages at speed, the algebra behind the sutras, and a timed capstone measured against week 1.

month_5_fractions_decimals_percentages.title: Month 5: Fractions, Decimals and Percentages at Speed

week_17.title: Recurring Decimals the Short Way
topics: Ekadhikena Purvena on fractions: 1/19 written out in seconds
The one-more-than-the-previous multiplier, forwards and backwards
1/7 and the cyclic pattern family
Auxiliary fractions: converting awkward denominators before dividing
Terminating or recurring: reading the denominator to know in advance

projects: Decimal expansion chart: 1/7, 1/13, 1/17, 1/19 expanded by sutra and verified by flag division
week_17.practice: 15 recurring-decimal expansions by the Ekadhikena method, each verified against straight division

week_18.title: Fraction Arithmetic Without the Grind
topics: Adding and subtracting fractions crosswise, no LCM hunt for two terms
When the LCM is still worth finding, honestly
Comparing fractions instantly by cross multiplication
Multiplying and dividing mixed numbers with vinculum thinking
Simplifying early: spotting common factors before multiplying
Fraction chains: working three-term expressions left to right
projects: Worked set: 20 fraction additions and comparisons done crosswise with times against the textbook method
week_18.practice: 30 mixed fraction operations, target under 15 minutes, five of them written both ways to compare

week_19.title: Percentages and Ratio Shortcuts
topics: Percentages as fractions: the 12.5 percent equals one-eighth family
Flipping the sum: 8 percent of 50 as 50 percent of 8
Percentage change chains done mentally
Ratio splitting at speed for word problems
Profit, loss and simple interest set up as one-line calculations
Estimation guards: catching a wrong percentage before it costs marks
projects: Percent-fraction equivalence card: the conversion table every student memorises, self-tested twice
week_19.practice: 40 mental percentage and ratio questions, target under 12 minutes, no written working allowed

week_20.title: Decimals and Speed Lab Three
topics: Multiplying decimals with base methods: 9.8 x 9.7 via 98 x 97
Placing the decimal point by estimation, not rules memorised blind
Dividing decimals with the flag method
Mixed arithmetic under time: the full toolkit on one paper
Error triage across fraction, decimal and percentage work
Month 5 consolidation chart
projects: Speed lab paper three: a timed 50-question fractions, decimals and percentages paper, marked and logged
week_20.practice: Redo all speed lab misses untimed, then a 20-question timed set on your two weakest question types
week_20.assessment: Month 5 milestone: timed mixed arithmetic paper, logged against the phase baselines

month_6_algebra_and_capstone.title: Month 6: Algebra Applications and the Speed-Test Capstone

week_21.title: The Sutras Meet Algebra
topics: Urdhva-Tiryagbhyam on binomials: (x + 3)(x + 7) crosswise
Multiplying larger polynomials by the same cross pattern
Paravartya for dividing polynomials by (x - a)
The connection made explicit: the arithmetic sutras were algebra all along
Checking polynomial products with substitution
Where this helps in school algebra chapters
projects: Bridge sheet: five arithmetic shortcuts and their algebraic twins worked side by side
week_21.practice: 20 polynomial multiplications and 10 Paravartya polynomial divisions, checked by substitution at x equals 2

week_22.title: Equations at Speed
topics: Sunyam Samyasamuccaye: spotting when a common factor makes the answer zero
One-line solutions to equation types that usually take five lines
Simultaneous equations by cross-multiplication patterns
Anurupye Sunyamanyat: proportional coefficients and what they force
Recognising equation types on sight before solving
Honest limits: equations where the shortcuts do not apply

week_22.practice: 25 equations mixing shortcut types and standard types, with the method named before each solution

week_23.title: Choosing the Right Tool
topics: Method triage: 40 mixed questions, decide the method in five seconds each
Exam mapping: where each technique saves time in school and MCQ papers
Where standard methods win and showing full working is required
Building your personal shortcut handbook: what goes in, what gets dropped
Mock speed test under full exam conditions
Reviewing the mock: method choices, not just answers
projects: Personal shortcut handbook draft: every technique the student actually uses, in their own words and examples
week_23.practice: Full 60-question mock speed test under timing, then a written half-page on your three best method choices

week_24.title: The Capstone Speed Test
topics: Final timed paper: same structure as the week 1 diagnostic, full toolkit allowed
Before and after: reading your speed log across all 24 weeks
Accuracy audit: error rate then versus now
Finishing the shortcut handbook: the take-away artifact of the course
Keeping the speed: a 10-minute weekly maintenance routine
Where to go next: exam-prep courses that use these skills daily
projects: Capstone speed test with a written before-and-after comparison against the week 1 diagnostic
Finished personal shortcut handbook, reviewed by the teacher

week_24.assessment: Course milestone: capstone speed test and handbook review, plus course-completion certificate review

weekly_structure.review: Drill sheets reviewed with error triage: every miss classified as method, carry or rush, and drilled accordingly

prerequisites.maths_level: Comfortable addition and subtraction, and multiplication tables up to 10. That is genuinely all

prerequisites.equipment: A notebook, a pen, and a device with a camera and stable internet for live classes
prerequisites.prior_vedic_maths: None expected. The course starts from the first sutra

who_is_this_for.school_students: Students in classes 3 to 10 who lose exam time to slow arithmetic and want it back
who_is_this_for.competitive_exam_aspirants: Older students preparing for arithmetic-heavy MCQ exams where a saved minute per section matters
who_is_this_for.parents_learning_alongside: Adults who want to sharpen mental arithmetic, sometimes in the same batch as their child
who_is_this_for.students_who_fear_calculation: Children who understand concepts but freeze on big multiplications; the drills rebuild confidence with evidence
who_is_this_for.maths_enjoyers: Students who simply like numbers and want to know why the shortcuts work, not just that they do

career_paths_after_completion: Faster arithmetic that pays off directly in school maths exams from class 4 upward
A head start for competitive-exam arithmetic sections, where calculation speed buys thinking time
Natural progression to our school maths and exam-prep courses, with calculation no longer the bottleneck
A strong base for algebra, since Urdhva-Tiryagbhyam and Paravartya reappear there directly
For adults: everyday numeracy, from splitting bills to sense-checking figures at work

course_guarantees.live_classes: Live, interactive classes with a real instructor, never pre-recorded videos.
course_guarantees.small_batches: Small batches only: group classes are capped at 10 students, with mini-batch (3 to 4 students) and personal 1-on-1 options.
course_guarantees.structured_curriculum: A structured, well-paced curriculum taught step by step, with hands-on practice in every session.
course_guarantees.doubt_support: Doubt support between classes over WhatsApp, so you are never left stuck.
course_guarantees.certificate: A course-completion certificate you can share.
course_guarantees.free_demo: A free demo class before you enrol, so you can decide with no pressure.

faqs.question: What is Vedic maths, honestly?
faqs.answer: It is a system of 16 sutras, short calculation rules, compiled by Bharati Krishna Tirthaji and published in 1965. Historians debate how ancient the system really is, and we say so in class, because the techniques stand on their own: they are fast, they are checkable, and every one of them has ordinary algebra underneath. We teach the methods and the algebra, not the mythology.

faqs.question: Will this replace my child's school maths?
faqs.answer: No, and it should not. School maths teaches concepts and requires standard working in answers; this course makes the arithmetic inside those answers faster and more accurate. We explicitly teach when to use a shortcut, when to show the standard method, and how to use digit-sum checks on both. Think of it as removing the calculation bottleneck from maths a student is already learning.

faqs.question: Does Vedic maths actually help in competitive exams?
faqs.answer: It helps with one specific thing: calculation speed in arithmetic-heavy sections, which frees minutes for the questions that need real thought. It does not teach exam concepts, shortcuts for reasoning, or anything a syllabus tests directly. Students preparing for MCQ exams tend to feel the benefit most in percentages, fractions and multiplication-heavy questions, which is exactly where phase 3 of this course spends its time.

faqs.question: Will this course make my child a genius or a human calculator?
faqs.answer: No, and we would rather tell you that upfront. What steady practice on this course produces is documented in each student's speed log: the same kinds of sums done faster and with fewer errors than in week 1. That is a real, useful, measurable gain. Claims beyond that, about IQ or brain development, are not ones we make.

faqs.question: What age is right for this course?
faqs.answer: We take students from age 8, once multiplication tables are reasonably solid, and there is no upper limit; adults join to sharpen mental arithmetic, sometimes in the same house as an enrolled child. Batches are grouped by age so an 8-year-old drills at a different pace from a 16-year-old preparing for entrance exams.

faqs.question: How is Vedic maths different from abacus classes?
faqs.answer: Abacus training builds mental arithmetic through a physical tool and bead visualisation, and it works best when started young, roughly ages 5 to 10. Vedic maths is paper-and-head arithmetic built on algebraic patterns, so it suits any age that knows its tables and extends naturally into algebra. Younger children often do abacus first and Vedic maths later; we run both and will honestly tell you which fits your child.

faqs.question: My child already struggles with maths. Is a speed course a bad idea?
faqs.answer: Often it is the opposite, with one condition: the struggles must be with calculation, not with concepts. A child who understands what multiplication means but dreads big numbers usually gains confidence quickly here, because the methods are short and the progress is visible in their own log. If the gaps are conceptual, we will say so after the demo class and point you to our school maths courses instead.

faqs.question: What does the course cost?
faqs.answer: ₹1,499 per month for group classes with 2 live classes weekly and at most 10 students per batch. Mini batches of 3 to 4 students are ₹2,499 per month, and personal 1-on-1 classes are ₹4,999 per month. International students pay $100 per month for group classes and $150 per month for 1-on-1.

faqs.question: Can we try a class before enrolling?
faqs.answer: Yes, the first demo class is free and carries no obligation. Book it at learn.modernagecoders.com/contact or message us on WhatsApp at +91 91233 66161. Parents are welcome to sit in, and the teacher will give you a straight opinion on whether this course or a school maths course is the better fit.
